name: Meta-Tracing Frameworks
summary: RPython meta-tracer builds a JIT for an interpreter rather than for a program: trace the interpreter's execution as it runs the program, partial-evaluate against the trace, and produce a specialised JIT for that program. PyPy is the production demonstration; 2SOM is the multi-tier example.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
**RPython** (the implementation language behind PyPy) provides a meta-tracing framework: a JIT generator that produces a tracing JIT for an interpreter rather than for a program. The interpreter is written in RPython; the meta-tracer traces the interpreter's execution as it runs the user program, then partial-evaluates against the trace to produce specialised native code.

This is Futamura's first projection in production form: the JIT is derived from the interpreter, not hand-written. Adding a new language to PyPy means writing only the interpreter; the JIT comes for free.

sections.title: 2SOM — adding tiers without a second backend
sections.content:
**2SOM** is a research artifact that demonstrates adding a warm-up tier to a meta-traced JIT without a second full backend. The technique: the same RPython interpreter is given multiple definitions at different levels of detail; the meta-tracer can trace any of them. The fast-path interpreter handles cold code; a more detailed interpreter handles warm code; the JIT specialises against the trace of whichever interpreter the meta-tracer recorded.

This avoids the cost of writing a second JIT backend while still getting tier-up behaviour. The cost is design effort to maintain multiple correct interpreter definitions; the benefit is much less engineering than building a full method-level optimising JIT alongside the trace JIT.

sources.label: A Lightweight Method for Generating Multi-Tier JIT
